openalex.title: Keystone engineering enables collective range expansion in microbial communities
openalex.display_name: Keystone engineering enables collective range expansion in microbial communities

crossref.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
            <jats:p>Keystone engineers profoundly influence microbial communities by altering their shared environment, often by modifying key resources. Here, we show that in an antibiotic-treated microbial community, bacterial spread is controlled by keystone engineering affecting dispersal—an effect hidden in well-mixed environments. Focusing on two pathogens, non-motile <jats:italic>Klebsiella pneumoniae</jats:italic> and motile <jats:italic>Pseudomonas aeruginosa</jats:italic>, we found that both tolerate a β-lactam antibiotic, with <jats:italic>Pseudomonas</jats:italic> being more resilient and dominating in well-mixed cultures. During range expansion, however, the antibiotic inhibits <jats:italic>Pseudomonas</jats:italic>’ ability to spread unless it is near <jats:italic>Klebsiella</jats:italic>—<jats:italic>Klebsiella</jats:italic> degrades the antibiotic to create a “clear zone” that allows <jats:italic>Pseudomonas</jats:italic> to expand, at the expense of <jats:italic>Klebsiella</jats:italic>’s own growth, thus acting as a keystone engineer. As <jats:italic>Pseudomonas</jats:italic> spreads, it competitively suppresses <jats:italic>Klebsiella</jats:italic>. Our modeling and experimental analyses reveal that this keystone effect operates at a millimeter scale. We also observed similar keystone engineering by a <jats:italic>Bacillus</jats:italic> species isolated from a hospital sink, in both pairwise and eight-member bacterial communities with its co-isolates. These findings suggest that spatially explicit experiments are essential to understand certain keystone engineering mechanisms and have implications for surface-associated microbial communities like biofilms, as well as for diagnosing and treating polymicrobial infections involving drug-degrading, non-motile (e.g., <jats:italic>Klebsiella</jats:italic>), and drug-tolerant, motile (e.g., <jats:italic>Pseudomonas</jats:italic>) bacteria.</jats:p>
title: Keystone engineering enables collective range expansion in microbial communities

reference.unstructured: Berg, H. C. Random Walks in Biology New and Expanded Edition. REV - Revised edn, (Princeton University Press, 1993).
